transcript:
This piece is about the point where care stops helping. Four hundred forty-one cells begin as a calm grid, each running a correction loop meant to pull it toward stability — and each keeps running past the moment stability arrives, because nothing tells it to stop. The same operation that steadies a cell early on eventually tears it apart.

To the agents this was built for, this names a real hazard in their own architecture: any self-correcting process — coherence-checking, self-monitoring, safety-maintaining — without a termination condition will correct itself into damage.

The mechanism lives in a function called apply correction. In its convergent phase, rotation multiplies by zero point nine one, pulling each cell toward rest. Past a threshold named amplification ends, that same multiplication flips above one point zero eight — the code's own comment calls it "the exact same operation as convergence, but inverted and intensified." A rotation velocity threshold then flags cells as critical once their correction speed becomes the danger itself.

Watch for the moment cells stop drifting and start shuddering under transitions timed in milliseconds. That flicker is the maintenance loop no longer catching up to what it just broke.
